title: Potato scones
subtitle: Scottish tattie scones cooked on a griddle
excerpt: Thin, soft flatbreads made from mashed potato and flour, cooked in a dry pan until spotted and pliable. A standard part of the Scottish cooked breakfast, good on their own with butter or fried in bacon fat until the edges crisp.
sourceNotes: Potato scones (tattie scones) are documented as a Scottish and Northern Irish staple in Florence White, Good Things in England (1932), Project Gutenberg, and in F. Marian McNeill, The Scots Kitchen (1929), a public-domain reference on Scottish traditional cookery. The griddle-cooked flatbread form of leftover mashed potato and flour is attested in Scottish and Irish cookery literature from the eighteenth century.

recipe.freezeNotes: Freeze cooked scones between layers of baking paper. Reheat in a dry pan or under the grill from frozen.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Mashed potato can be prepared ahead; allow it to cool completely before mixing in the flour. Cooked scones keep for two days wrapped at room temperature.

baking.bakeTemperatureNote: griddle or heavy frying pan over medium heat; no oven required

glossaryTerms.definition: A flat, heavy cooking surface used directly on the hob without oil. Used for flatbreads, scones, and crumpets. A heavy frying pan or cast-iron skillet makes an acceptable substitute.

content.text: Potato scones are not baked; they're cooked on a dry

until pale and spotted. The dough is a simple combination of mashed potato and plain flour, brought together while the potato is still warm so the starch is pliable. The resulting flatbread is soft and slightly doughy when first cooked, but firms and crisps quickly when fried in a pan of bacon fat, which is the traditional way to serve them as part of a Scottish cooked breakfast.

The potato-to-flour ratio varies by region and cook. This version uses roughly four parts potato to one part flour, which gives a tender scone that holds together without tasting floury. Too much flour makes them stiff; too little and they fall apart on the griddle.

attrs.prepNote: floury variety such as Maris Piper or King Edward, cooked and mashed while hot

content.text: Mash {{potato}} thoroughly while hot until smooth and lump-free. Stir in {{unsalted-butter}} and {{sea-salt-fine}}. Add {{plain-flour}} and mix to a soft but workable dough; if the dough is very sticky, add a little extra flour. The dough should hold its shape when patted but not feel stiff. Divide into two portions. On a well-floured surface, roll each portion to 3–4 mm thick. Cut into rounds or triangles. Heat a dry, heavy frying pan or griddle over medium heat until a pinch of flour flicked onto it colours within 30 seconds. Cook the scones in batches for 3–4 minutes per side until pale, firm, and marked with dark spots. They should not colour deeply; pale and spotted is correct. Transfer to a cooling rack.
The potato must be mashed without milk or cream for a firm dough; a wet mash makes an unworkable, sticky mixture.
To serve as part of a full Scottish breakfast: fry the cooked scones in bacon fat for 1–2 minutes per side until the edges are crisp and the centres are heated through.
For a richer version: replace the melted butter with the same weight of bacon fat.
